pages.full_text:
& mediaeval-looking, with all the people & bustle & bright colours, groups of people stopping to chat & buskers. We walked through the south gate, Stonebow, early C16, originally the Roman gate. Further up the [?Steep] hill is the Jews House, very pretty.
      The Cathedral is being cleaned. It's very beautiful. 1072-[?12], homogenous architecture, the third largest in England. (We didn't have time to visit the nearby castle 1065) There is a quiet adult chapel for private prayer, & also a prayer corner where you can light candles, put them in sand in tris [a more friendly place for children]. Thomas & Peter paid their money & there were no candles left, so they had to ask a flunky for more. Also there are 4½ ft. high candlesticks, made at the limit of the potter's wheel & firing. One of these had barbed wire around it.
pages.notes: Page begins mid-sentence, continuing from a previous page about Lincoln. 'Stonebow' is the south gate of Lincoln, early C16. The number after '1072-' is partially obscured. The marginalia 'a more friendly place for children' appears to be a later annotation in smaller text. 'tris' may be short for 'trays' or similar. No year can be confirmed from this page alone; context suggests a Lincoln visit sometime in the 1990s based on volume position.

pages.full_text:
An earthquake split the original cathedral & St. Hugh, a Carthusian monk from Avalon & Burgundy, rebuilt it. "He was a man of great energy, powerful mind & inflexible purpose."
 It was built right through the original Roman wall. It is recorded that when Hugh died, the Jews wept at his funeral. His shrine was finished by 1280.
      There is also a huge bronze statue of Tennyson 1809-92 by G.F. Watts on the Chapter house green.
      We had lunch at Pimento – good stuffed peppers, & caught the bus back to the van. After tea we all went swimming, even Charles, who was only just over his chickenpox.
      At 9.30. on the Sunday we reached the Cathedral for the main Sunday service. Unfortunately, the choir was away. We were squashed
pages.notes: Top of page shows '1185' which is a historical date (the earthquake that split Lincoln Cathedral). Handwritten page number '119' visible in top-right corner. 'Pimento' is a restaurant/cafe in Lincoln. Page ends mid-sentence, continues on next spread. 'Avalon' here likely refers to Avalon in Burgundy (Saint-Étienne d'Avallon), not the legendary Avalon. St. Hugh of Lincoln (c.1140-1200) was indeed a Carthusian monk. G.F. Watts is the sculptor George Frederic Watts. No year can be confirmed from this page alone.
